These fit naturally within the broader "Reference" category but could motivate distinguishing nominal reference from pronominal coreference in future refinements. Many dialogue excerpts without tags rely on speaker persistence across adjacent turns, reinforcing the importance of the Speaker Continuity category. Taxonomy assessment The current taxonomy appears sufficient for this batch. The only recurring refinement worth considering after annotating the full dataset is whether Reference: Coreference and Aliasing should be divided into: Pronominal Coreference, and Nominal/Alias Reference, as these appear to represent distinct surface phenomena despite requiring similar explicit resolution capabilities. This is actually a very useful comparison. I think this second annotation set is overall stronger than mine, but there are also a few places where I think it over-attributes failures to Coreference. I'll go through the major patterns. --- Overall Verdict | Aspect                           | Mine   | Second Set | Better          | | -------------------------------- | ------ | ---------- | --------------- | | Follows coding manual            | 8.5/10 | 9.5/10     | Second          | | Separates discourse vs reference | 8/10   | 8.5/10     | Slightly Second | | Identifies alias failures        | 7/10   | 10/10      | Second          | | Uses evidence consistently       | 8/10   | 10/10      | Second          | | Conservative use of pragmatics   | 9/10   | 8/10       | Mine            | | Notes explain mechanism          | 8.5/10 | 9.5/10     | Second          | I'd estimate agreement around 80–85%, with the disagreements being scientifically interesting rather than obvious mistakes. --- Where the second set is clearly better 1. Alias vs String Matching Example: > "said the King" I labeled this as: > Reference but didn't explicitly recognize the likely alias mismatch: ``` the King ↓ The King Of Hearts ``` The second annotator correctly identifies that this is not a discourse problem but an alias/normalization problem. I would adopt their annotation. --- 2. "the former" Quote 909 I labeled > Speaker Continuity The second annotator says > Reference > Evidence: discourse marker I think they're right. "The former" is an explicit referring expression. Your taxonomy literally defines: > pronoun, alias, discourse marker So this belongs in Reference. --- 3. Relational aliases Example ``` said her mother ``` I treated it almost like an alias. The second annotator explicitly notes > relational alias That's a really nice observation. It might become an important subtype later. --- Where I think the second set overuses Coreference There are several places where I would disagree. --- Quote 644 ``` she exclaimed ``` Second says Primary = Coreference This is defensible. But here's the question from your coding manual: > Why did the explicit representation fail? If the representation already encodes ``` speaker = previous speaker ``` then the pronoun becomes almost irrelevant. I think this one is genuinely debatable. --- Quote 400 ``` she addressed him a second time ``` Second: Primary = Coreference I think this misses something. The difficult part is ``` a second time ``` which implies discourse continuity. Resolving "she" is easy. Knowing who is speaking again is harder. I'd probably still lean ``` Speaker Continuity Secondary = Coreference ``` --- Quote 640 ``` he added in an undertone to the Queen ``` Second: Primary = Coreference I actually disagree. The interesting reasoning is ``` to the Queen ``` That is conversational. The pronoun "he" is almost trivial. I'd still favor Primary = Pragmatics --- Quote 535 This is the biggest disagreement. The second annotator says Primary = Coreference because ``` she said to him ``` I don't think that's the difficult part. The difficult part is Who is speaking to whom? This is exactly what your taxonomy defines as Speaker–Addressee Semantics So I still prefer Pragmatics. --- Another observation The second annotator is using ``` Explicit Alternative Feasible? No ``` for almost everything. I don't think that's consistent with the coding manual. For example, ``` said the King ``` could absolutely become ``` Speaker = King Of Hearts ``` through preprocessing. That's explicitly feasible. I think they interpreted > feasible as > currently possible instead of > theoretically representable without semantics. --- The biggest methodological difference The second annotator seems to classify > What linguistic object caused the failure? Whereas your coding manual says to classify > What missing capability would solve the failure? Those are subtly different. Example: ``` she replied ``` Linguistic object: > pronoun Missing capability: > discourse state > or > coreference depending on context. That's exactly the distinction your manual emphasizes. --- One thing I would absolutely steal I really like this observation: > split Reference into ``` Pronoun Resolution Lexical Normalization / Alias Matching ``` That's an excellent idea. Your current taxonomy combines two quite different phenomena: semantic reference resolution (pronouns, discourse markers like "the former") surface normalization (Mrs Miller ↔ Mrs. Miller, the King ↔ King of Hearts) The former is a linguistic reasoning problem; the latter is often just preprocessing. Separating them would make your analysis much more informative. --- My recommendation If this second annotation set was produced by another LLM (e.g., Gemini), I would not simply choose one annotator over the other. Instead, I'd use these disagreements to refine the coding manual before annotating the remaining batches. The recurring disagreements reveal that the manual still leaves room for interpretation, particularly around the boundary between Reference and Discourse: Speaker Continuity. Tightening those decision rules now will improve consistency across the rest of the dataset and produce a stronger final annotation corpus.

Batch 004 Summary Most common category: Discourse: Speaker Continuity (dominant across the batch) Any new phenomena observed: No genuinely new linguistic phenomena emerged. Several examples involve interior monologue ("thought she", "said to herself"), but these are well captured by Speaker Continuity with Narrative Focus evidence rather than requiring a new taxonomy category. Courtroom dialogue in Alice's Adventures in Wonderland repeatedly reinforces that institutional discourse roles (e.g., the King presiding) are naturally handled under Speaker Continuity. Whether the current taxonomy appears sufficient or requires refinement: The taxonomy remains largely sufficient for this batch. One refinement worth considering in future iterations is clarifying in the coding manual that narrative-focus constructions (e.g., "thought she", "said to herself", long reporting clauses before quotations) should generally default to Discourse: Speaker Continuity, with Pronominal Coreference used only when pronoun resolution is the actual bottleneck. This distinction would likely improve inter-annotator consistency. Compare results This is actually a useful comparison because it reveals a systematic philosophical difference between the two annotation styles rather than random disagreement. I'll refer to Set A as my annotations and Set B as the JSON annotations. Overall Agreement Category	Agreement Primary Category	17/25 (68%) Secondary Category	~80% Evidence	~88% Context Window	~72% Confidence	~88% The disagreements are concentrated in 8 quotes and almost all stem from one issue: Should an explicit reporting expression ("said she", "he replied", "Mr Collins replied", "the King said") always be treated as a Reference problem, or should we ask why that explicit cue still failed? Your coding manual explicitly says: Always ask: "Why did the explicit contextual representation fail?" That question is crucial. Major Disagreements 1. PP_811 Mine Speaker Continuity Secondary: Coreference JSON Coreference I would probably concede this one. The only missing capability is resolving she. This is a genuine coreference case. Winner: JSON 2. Alice_15 Mine Speaker Continuity JSON Coreference Again I think JSON is stronger. The narration literally says She ate... said to herself The bottleneck is resolving She. Winner: JSON 3. Alice_222 Same reasoning. Again I would switch to Coreference. 4. PP_450 Mine Speaker Continuity JSON Coreference Here I actually disagree with JSON. The narration is He took the hint... when Mary had finished... said aloud This is not simply resolving "He". The discourse focus has been maintained over a long narration. The pronoun is trivial. The hard part is maintaining the narrative subject. I still prefer Speaker Continuity 5. PP_736 Mine Speaker Continuity JSON Alias Matching This is the most interesting disagreement. The quote begins Mr Collins was gratified... Candidate: Mr. Collins If your explicit system performs literal string matching, then Mr Collins vs Mr. Collins is indeed a preprocessing problem. That is exactly why you introduced Lexical Normalization / Alias Matching I actually think JSON is correct here. 6. PP_286 Exactly the same. Mr Darcy vs Mr. Darcy Again this is lexical normalization. JSON wins. 7. Alice_536 Mine Speaker–Addressee Semantics JSON Coreference I think mine is stronger. Why? The narration already says he said to the Gryphon The pronoun isn't difficult. The important information is to the Gryphon because speaker identity is inferred from the interaction. I would keep Pragmatics. 8. Alice_443 Mine Pragmatics JSON Alias Matching I disagree with JSON. Lowercase the Queen vs The Queen isn't really an alias. It's capitalization. Your coding manual defines alias matching as title spelling variation alias Not capitalization. Furthermore the Queen said to Alice contains an explicit addressee. The pragmatic interaction is more informative than capitalization. I prefer Pragmatics. 9. Alice_645 Mine Speaker Continuity JSON Alias Matching This one is genuinely difficult. Dialogue tag said the King Candidate The King Of Hearts The failure really could be solved by King → King Of Hearts dictionary. I lean toward JSON. 10. Alice_620 Same. Probably Alias Matching. Biggest Pattern This is the most important finding. JSON consistently assumes If there is an explicit lexical cue, classify the lexical cue. Your coding manual explicitly warns against this. It says Ask why the explicit contextual representation failed. Those are different philosophies. One weakness in my annotations I underused 1b Alias Matching After seeing this batch I think I was too conservative. Cases like Mr Darcy vs Mr. Darcy really are lexical normalization. That is exactly why category 1b exists. I would update my future annotations accordingly. One weakness in the JSON annotations JSON appears to overuse 1a Coreference Every she said becomes Coreference even when Speaker Continuity is clearly the harder reasoning step. Your own coding manual says Do not overuse Coreference. Some examples (e.g. PP_450) illustrate this issue well. Overall Assessment If these JSON annotations came from Gemini, I would score the comparison as: Agreement rate: ~68% on primary category. Gemini strengths: Better recognition of lexical normalization (Category 1b); more consistent identification of straightforward pronoun-resolution cases. My strengths: Better adherence to the principle of identifying the primary missing reasoning capability, particularly for long narrative passages where discourse continuity outweighs surface lexical cues. Coding manual refinement suggested: Clarify when an explicit reporting expression like "said she" should be coded as Pronominal Coreference versus Speaker Continuity. A practical decision rule could be: If resolving the pronoun alone identifies the speaker, use 1a Reference: Pronominal Coreference. If the pronoun is trivial but maintaining discourse or narrative state is the real challenge, use 2. Discourse: Speaker Continuity. This single clarification would likely eliminate most of the disagreements observed in this batch.
